coredata.dc:title: In situ construction of ridge-like PVDF/TiO2/CQDs hybrid film with multiple electron transfer channels based on S-scheme heterostructure and dynamic "piezo-photocatalytic" mechanism for dye removal
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Zihan
Xu, Yumei
Liu, Qingyu
Wu, Xiao
Qin, Shuhao
Zhang, Jing
Wang, Hong
Li, Jianxin
Cui, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: The ridge-like PVDF/CQDs-TiO2@CQDs hybrid piezo-photocatalytic film was fabricated via an "in situ hydrothermal-vapor induction" method, in which the S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures were grown in situ and uniformly immobilization onto the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film surface through Ti-F coordination bonds. The effects of hybrid film morphology, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doping, and flow rate on piezo-photocatalytic degradation performance were investigated. The ridge-like microstructure increased the exposure of adsorption and catalytic active sites. The film reactor driven by the flow of dye solution and light illumination promoted the adsorption of pollutants while establishing a dual built-in electric field (IEF) between the piezoelectric PVDF and catalysts, TiO2 and CQDs, which accelerated photogenerated charge transfer and separation. Finally, a dynamic piezo-photocatalytic process was realized for the efficient and synergistic degradation of RhB. The reaction rate constant (ks) of the dynamic piezo-photocatalytic degradation was about 2.3 times that of the static photocatalytic process. Structural optimization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations of Rhodamine B (RhB) molecules were performed using Gaussian software. The degradation products, degradation pathways, and degradation mechanisms of RhB were thoroughly studied by combining the results of total organic carbon (TOC), DFT and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS).

head.ce:title: In situ construction of ridge-like PVDF/TiO2/CQDs hybrid film with multiple electron transfer channels based on S-scheme heterostructure and dynamic "piezo-photocatalytic" mechanism for dye removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ridge-like PVDF/CQDs-TiO2@CQDs hybrid piezo-photocatalytic film was fabricated via an "in situ hydrothermal-vapor induction" method, in which the S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures were grown in situ and uniformly immobilization onto the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film surface through Ti-F coordination bonds. The effects of hybrid film morphology, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doping, and flow rate on piezo-photocatalytic degradation performance were investigated. The ridge-like microstructure increased the exposure of adsorption and catalytic active sites. The film reactor driven by the flow of dye solution and light illumination promoted the adsorption of pollutants while establishing a dual built-in electric field (IEF) between the piezoelectric PVDF and catalysts, TiO2 and CQDs, which accelerated photogenerated charge transfer and separation. Finally, a dynamic piezo-photocatalytic process was realized for the efficient and synergistic degradation of RhB. The reaction rate constant (ks) of the dynamic piezo-photocatalytic degradation was about 2.3 times that of the static photocatalytic process. Structural optimization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations of Rhodamine B (RhB) molecules were performed using Gaussian software. The degradation products, degradation pathways, and degradation mechanisms of RhB were thoroughly studied by combining the results of total organic carbon (TOC), DFT and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS).

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a clean advanced oxidation technology, photocatalysis is an ideal strategy for decomposing organic pollutants in water without secondary pollution [1]. Nano-TiO2 is the ideal photocatalytic material due to its photostability, eco-friendliness, non-toxic, and low cost [2]. However, the utilization of visible light of TiO2 is limited by its wide band gap, and inherent agglomeration properties [3]. In addition, nano-TiO2 is challenging to be recycled and reused due to the fact that they are usually dispersed in pollutants, which limits their application.

Different strategies had been developed to increase the photochemical properties of TiO2, such as elemental doping [4], metal cocatalyst [5], heterostructure construction [6], and morphological control [7]. Among them, constructing semiconductor heterostructures has attracted much attention because it can effectively inhibit electron-hole recombination and improve solar energy utilization through the built-in electric field (IEF), thus enhancing the photocatalytic activities. Quantum dots, nanosheets, and individual metal atoms had been used as effective sensitizers for the construction of semiconductor heterostructures due to their tunable bandgap and quantum confinement effects [8]. In particular, as an inexpensive metal-free carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) had drawn much attention for their excellent photogenerated electron storage capacity and good interfacial electron transfer efficiency [9,10]. It has been reported that the photocatalytic abilities of TiO2 could be effectively enhanced by heterostructures constructed via coupling CQDs. Wang et al. [11] and Tong et al. [12] both successfully synthesized composite nanoparticles of CQDs/TiO2, which improved light absorption ability, and thus achieved the effective degradation of RhB. Furthermore, to address the issues of easy agglomeration and difficult recycling of powdered catalysts in the actual water treatment process, some research groups, including our group, reported that the construction of hybrid film materials by loading TiO2 onto polymeric film carriers (such as polysulfone (PSF), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), etc.) could realize effective dispersion of the photocatalysts and enhance their long-term and reusable stability in water treatment [13–16]. However, polymer films are usually used as inert media when applied as photocatalyst carriers, because they only play the role of loading rather than participating in the catalytic degradation reaction, thereby limiting the improvement of the catalytic efficiency. Therefore, it is necessary to enable the polymer film to play a catalytic role in polymer-based hybrid materials.

The β-phase PVDF film can generate piezoelectric potentials as it exhibits obvious strains under low stress, thus induce the generation of polarization fields or charges when fluids containing organic pollutants flow over the film surface, which can directly drive the redox reactions [17,18] and realize the catalytic degradation of pollutants [19–21]. Moreover, the transport, separation, and reorganization of charges can be efficiently regulated by the IEF generated based on the piezoelectric effect [22]. Therefore, the flexible PVDF film can act as a TiO2 photocatalyst carrier and is expected to simultaneously combine its piezoelectric properties, thus overall boosting the catalytic degradation of pollutants in water by coupling PVDF film and TiO2.

However, some critical issues still face the polymer/TiO2 hybrid catalytic films. In detail, (1) How to simultaneously realize the construction of TiO2-based heterostructures with high catalytic performance and their uniform and firm loading on the polymer matrix? (2) The PVDF film prepared by immersed precipitated phase conversion is usually the non-polar α-phase with hardly piezoelectric properties. How to induce the transition of PVDF from α-phase to β-phase in a facile approach, so as to enhance the piezoelectric performance of the material and contribute to the catalytic degradation of pollutants? (3) The design and stable operation of the reactor are crucial in the actual wastewater treatment process. How to couple the photo-electric properties of materials and realize the long-term dynamic piezo-photocatalytic synergistic degradation for pollutants?

In order to address the above issues, firstly, the ridge-like PVDF/CQDs-TiO2@CQDs hybrid films with the synergistic effect of piezo-photocatalytic were constructed, in which the strong electronegativity F atoms and the CQDs introduced within the PVDF films provided the nucleation sites of TiO2 nanoparticles. The in situ construction and immobilization of S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures on the film surface were realized. The ridge-like structure ensured the exposure of catalytic sites. Secondly, the conversion of α-phase PVDF to piezoelectric β-phase was induced by the in situ doped of the CQDs, thus improved the photo-electric performance of the hybrid film. In addition, the surface morphologies, physicochemical structures, and specific surface area of the films were analyzed. The optical properties, charge transport properties, and heterostructure types were systematically explored. What's more, a dynamic piezo-photocatalytic reactor was designed and operated. The influence regulations of flow rate and the CQDs doping on the catalytic degradation of the dye were revealed. Finally, the degradation intermediates and pathways of RhB were confirmed by combining the density functional theory (DFT) calculations, total organic carbon (TOC) removal rate test, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis. The electron transfer and dynamic piezo-photocatalytic synergistic degradation mechanisms were revealed. This work proposes a new strategy for the facile fabrication of flexible "piezo-photocatalytic" film with superior catalytic performance and the operation of film reactors.

PVDF was provided by 3F New Materials Co. Ltd (China). Citric acid and urea were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) was bought from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). RhB, tetracycline, and ethanol (EtOH) were supplied by Tianjin Feng Chuan Chemical Reagents Factory (China).

First, citric acid (4.5 g) and urea (4.5 g) were mixed with 15 g of deionized water to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the solution was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 ºC for 6 h. Afterwards, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Then, the mixture was filtered and centrifuged to remove larger particles, dialysis for 48 h to remove impurities. Finally, the brownish-yellow solution was freeze-dried to obtain the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PVDF and PVDF/CQDs film matrices
ce:section.ce:para: The film matrices were obtained by the phase inversion method. Briefly, 0.75 g of PVDF was dissolved in DMAC (4.25 g). Then, the mixture was stirred continuously at 65 ℃ for 8 h. The obtained solution was degassed overnight. Next, a transparent flat sheet was created using a hand-casting blade on a clear glass support and transferred to water to form the final PVDF film. PVDF/CQDs film was also prepared by a similar procedure with only the 0.015 g addition of CQDs to the casting solution.

All the hybrid films were facilely fabricated by the "in situ hydrothermal-vapor induction" method (Fig. 1 ). In detail, 0.05 mL TBOT was mixed with 20 mL EtOH and sonicated for 0.5 h. Then, the mixed solution and a PVDF film or PVDF/CQDs film (4 cm × 4 cm) were placed in a 25 mL container. Then, the Teflon container was put in a 150 mL container with 6 mL of ultrapure water, which was subsequently placed in a stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 10 h to obtain PVDF-TiO2 (named P-T) and PVDF/CQDs-TiO2 (named PC-T) hybrid films, respectively. The PVDF-TiO2@CQDs and PVDF/CQDs-TiO2@CQDs hybrid films were prepared by using the same method only with the addition of CQDs (5 g L−1) in the TBOT/EtOH mixed solution. They were named P-TC and PC-TC, respectively. The TiO2 and TiO2@CQDs nanoparticles were also fabricated under similar conditions without film matrix addition. Meanwhile, hybrid films with different CQDs additions were produced to study the effect of CQDs doping on the catalytic degradation ability. Hybrid films with CQDs doping concentrations of 1 g L−1, 3 g L−1, and 9 g L−1 were named PC-TC1, PC-TC3, and PC-TC9, respectively.

The microstructure of the films was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS), and scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The charge separation efficiency was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and photoluminescence spectra (PL). The specific surface area of films was tested by a specific surface analyzer (BET, NOVA4200e). Piezo response force microscopy (PFM) was observed by a Bruker Dimension Icon. Detailed test methods were described in Supplementary Material.
Static and dynamic catalytic experiments were carried out to explore and compare the photocatalytic and piezo-photocatalytic degradation abilities of hybrid films.

The photocatalytic activities of the hybrid films (2 cm×2 cm, 26.6 mg) were tested using a 300 W xenon lamp (λ>420 nm). RhB (20 mg/L, 25 mL) and tetracycline (20 mg/L, 25 mL) acted as the target degradants. Prior to the degradation experiment, the mixed solution with the placed film was stirred under darkness for 1 h. The details are given in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The film reactor and dynamic piezo-photocatalytic experiment
ce:section.ce:para: The self-designed dynamic piezo-photocatalytic film reactor is shown in Fig. 2 . The film is laid flat and secured in a stainless-steel film cell with a transparent quartz glass cover to ensure that the film receives adequate light irradiation. The effective area of the film is 3.14 cm2 (20.9 mg). The dye solution was circulated for 60 min in a dark flow-through system prior to the catalytic reaction. In this dynamic reactor, the dye solution is stored in a feed tank and transported to the film cell by a peristaltic pump, where the dye solution flows on the film surface and exerts a certain pressure on the film, and the dynamic piezo-photocatalytic synergistic degradation for the dye is realized under the visible light (xenon lamp). The flow rate (24 mL min−1) of the dye solution was controlled by the peristaltic pump (Longer Pump, BT100–2 J, YZ1515x). The concentration and dosage of the solution were consistent with those of the static photocatalytic reaction. The optical photograph of the dynamic film reactor is shown in Fig. S1.

The FTIR results are presented in Fig. 3 . The broad peaks at 3200∼3500 cm−1 in Fig. 3(a) were the hydroxyl groups stretching vibrations of TiO2. The peak near 1643 cm−1 represented the C = O vibrations of CQDs, which indicated the in situ formation of TiO2 and the successful introduction of CQDs into the hybrid film [23]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the peaks at 1383 cm−1, 1182 cm−1, 765 cm−1, and 610 cm−1 corresponded to the nonpolar α-phase of PVDF, whereas the peaks located at 1073 cm−1 and 874 cm−1 corresponded to the β-phase with piezoelectric properties [24,25], which indicated that both α-phase and β-phase of PVDF were present in the prepared film. In addition, the peak signals at 1280 cm−1, 840 cm−1 and 510 cm−1 corresponding to β-phase PVDF were significantly strengthened in PC-T and PC-TC. This can be attributed to the fact that the dispersed phase with abundant surface defects of CQDs introduced during the preparation of PVDF/CQDs film provided a mass potential-induced active point for the generation of the β-phase PVDF, which facilitated the phase transformation of the PVDF [24,26].

The XRD patterns was shown in Fig. 3(c). The peak at around 18.2° corresponded to the (020) face of the α-phase PVDF, while the peak at around 20.6° corresponded to the (110)/ (200) face of the β-phase PVDF [27,28]. The characteristic peak signals of the β-phase in the PC-TC were obvious and exhibited clear piezoelectric properties. The appearance of the peak signal of anatase phase TiO2 at 25.3° indicated the in situ growth of the high catalytic active phase TiO2 on the film surface and the successful preparation of the hybrid film. The absence of characteristic peaks of CQDs was attributed to the overlap of the peaks near 25° corresponding to CQDs (002) and TiO2 (101) and the low addition of CQDs [29,30].

The XPS results (Fig. 3(d)) show that all the hybrid films contain the elements of F, O, Ti, and C. As depicted in Fig. 3(e) and (f), the F 1 s of the pristine PVDF film exhibited only a distinct single peak attributed to the single chemical environment of the F atom in PVDF. On the contrary, two characteristic peaks were distinctly detected in the F 1 s spectra of the hybrid films, which is evidence of the formation of Ti-F coordination bonds between the PVDF film matrix and TiO2 [31]. Strong electronegativity endowed the F − with electron-withdrawing ability from Ti4+, which in turn led to the formation of strong coordination bands between F − and Ti4+ (PVDF inner core and TiO2 shell) [32]. Thus, the firm immobilization of the catalyst on the polymer film was ensured. Moreover, the strong electronegativity of F endowed the Ti-F coordination bond with electron trapping ability, which established channels for photogenerated charge transfer of the catalyst [33,34].

TiO2 nanoparticles formed on the film surface by in situ growth can be seen from the SEM of hybrid films (Fig. 4 .). It can also be noticed that there were regions on the P-T surface where TiO2 is not present (Fig. 4(a, e)). The relatively flat surface and the uniform and dense distribution of TiO2 of P-TC (Fig. 4(b, f)) were ascribed to the introduction of CQDs in the TBOT precursor solution that promoted the nucleation and growth of TiO2 nanoparticles. In contrast, the PC-T (Fig. 4(c, g)) and PT-CT surfaces (Fig. 4(d, h)) exhibited wrinkled morphologies at the microscopic scale, as well as a dense distribution of TiO2. This can be attributed to the CQDs in the PVDF film matrix, which not only provided nucleation sites for TiO2 but also affected the microscopic morphologies of the hybrid film. As a result, the PC-TC film exhibited a unique ridge-like morphology. The elemental mapping of PC-TC (Fig. 4(i)) also corresponded to the ridge-like morphology and the homogeneous immobilization of TiO2 on the film surface, with a small amount of N elements detected belonging to CQDs.

The TiO2@CQDs nanoparticles were individually characterized by HR-TEM to understand the structure of the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure. As shown in Fig. 4(j) and 4(k), the diameter of CQDs was smaller about 3 nm, whereas the diameter of TiO2 was 11∼14 nm. For TiO2@CQDs (Fig. 4(l)), the CQDs were embedded into TiO2 and formed the tightly connected TiO2@CQDs heterostructures. However, the heterostructure nanoparticles exhibited severe agglomeration due to the absence of films as loading sites. The HR-TEM results of TiO2@CQDs (Fig. 4(m)) showed different lattice spacings of 0.352 nm and 0.273 nm, which indexed to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 and the (002) crystalline plane of CQDs, respectively. Additionally, two lattices with different orientations agglomerated at the junction, suggested a close interface interaction between TiO2 and CQDs was established [35]. The EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 4(n)) shows the distribution of C, O, and Ti elements of TiO2@CQDs, further confirming the construction of TiO2@CQDs heterostructures. As shown in the XPS results of TiO2@CQDs (Fig. S2 (a)), the enhancement of the C 1 s peak and the addition of the N elemental signal were ascribed to the CQDs. The peaks of TiO2 at 458.48 eV and 464.13 eV shown in the Ti 2p XPS spectra (Fig. S2(b)) can be assigned to Ti (2p 3/2) and Ti (2p 1/2), respectively. The XPS spectra of O 1 s (Fig. S2(c)) at 533.13 eV, 531.63 eV, and 530.03 eV corresponded to CO, OH, and Ti-O, respectively. Notably, the Ti 2p and O 1 s peaks of TiO2@CQDs shifted to higher binding energy positions compared to TiO2, with a shift of about 0.3 eV and 0.5 eV for the Ti 2p and O 1 s peaks, respectively. This suggests the change in the bonding environment of Ti and O due to the strong interaction force and Ti-O-C bonds between the CQDs and TiO2 [12,36–38]. The increase in elemental binding energy also implied the atom lost electrons and a decrease in electron density [39]. Therefore, it can be judged that electrons were transferred from TiO2 to CQDs after a tight interfacial contact was established between TiO2 and CQDs.

The electronic and energy band structures of TiO2@CQDs were explored in depth (Fig. 7 ). As can be found in the UV–vis DRS (Fig. 7(a, d)), TiO2 is only able to absorb UV light absorption below 420 nm, in contrast to CQDs, which exhibited stronger light harvesting in the visible light range (400∼700 nm). The Eg of TiO2 and CQDs were 3.15 eV and 2.81 eV, respectively (Fig. 7(b, e)). As shown in the valence band XPS (VB-XPS) spectra (Fig. 7 (c, f)), the valence band values of TiO2 and CQDs were 3.11 eV and 1.91 eV, respectively. The EVB of the corresponding standard hydrogen electrode (EVB vs. NHE) can be obtained by the formula: EVB vs. NHE = Φ + E VB − XPS − 4.44 [42], where Φ refers to the work function (4.20 eV). The calculated EVB of TiO2 and CQDs were 2.87 eV and 1.67 eV, respectively. According to the equation: Eg = E VB − ECB, the calculated ECB of TiO2 and CQDs were −0.28 eV and −1.14 eV, respectively. In addition, Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to characterize the work functions (Φ) of TiO2 and CQDs (Fig. S3). Φ was calculated using the equation [43]: Φ = hv − Ecutoff , where hv and Ecutoff are the incoming photon energy from the He I source (21.21 eV) and the secondary electron cutoff energy, respectively. The calculated Φ of TiO2 and CQDs were 7.31 eV and 6.29 eV. TiO2 has a large work function Φ and a low Fermi energy level (EF), while CQDs exhibit a small Φ and a high EF. Based on the above data, the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure is consistent with the S-scheme heterostructure [44]. The schematic diagram of the TiO2@CQDs heterostructure is shown in Fig. 7(g). As TiO2 and CQDs are in close contact, the free electrons in TiO2 will transfer to the CQDs until an equilibrium EF is reached. This charge redistribution effect at the interface results in the establishment of a built-in electric field (IEF) at the interface of tightly coupled TiO2 and CQDs. Furthermore, the band edges of TiO2 bend downward due to electrons aggregation, while the band edges of CQDs bend upward. Under light irradiation, photogenerated electrons (e −) were driven by IEF and band bending to transfer from the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 to the valence band (VB) of CQDs, which accelerated charge transfer and enhanced photoactivity.

The results of static photocatalytic experiments are shown in Fig. 8(a) . The P-T without the introduction of CQDs hardly degraded RhB due to the wide Eg and low visible light activity of TiO2. The degradation effect (η) of RhB by hybrid films descended in the order PC-TC > P-TC > PC-T > P-T. The significant enhancement of the degradation efficiency of PC-T was ascribed to the CQDs in the film matrix, which enhanced visible light absorption and promoted electron transfer between the film matrix and TiO2. The tightly connected S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructure was constructed by the in situ doping of CQDs during the TiO2 growth process, which promoted electron transfer more efficiently and further enhanced the η of P-TC. The multiple electron transfer channels established by the CQDs embedded within the film matrix and TiO2, the S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructure, and the Ti-F bonds of the PC-TC endowed it with the highest photocatalytic performance. The η of PC-TC for RhB reached 85.31 % within 120 min. The concentrations of the RhB at the time of degradation are shown in Table S1. The RhB degradation process was evaluated using the Langmuir-Hinshlwood kinetic model (ln(c t /c 0) = −kt). The c0 and ct represent the initial and real-time concentrations of the RhB solution, respectively. k is the apparent rate constant. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the calculated k values for P-T, PC-T, P-TC, and PC-TC were 0.0006 min−1, 0.006 min−1, 0.008 min−1 and 0.015 min−1, respectively. For photocatalytic film materials, the reaction rate constant (ks) can be evaluated from the following equation [45]:
k = k s
A V
where A (cm2) and V (mL) represent the film area and the volume of the dye solution. The ks can be used to evaluate the photocatalytic capacity per unit film area at a certain light intensity and initial dye solution concentration. The calculated ks values were 0.004 mL min−1 cm−2 (P-T), 0.038 mL min−1 cm−2 (PC-T), 0.05 mL min−1 cm−2 (P-TC), and 0.094 mL min−1 cm−2 (PC-TC).

The piezoelectric constant (Table S4) of PC-TC (24.6 PC N − 1) significantly enhanced compared to the pristine PVDF film (13.6 PC N − 1). It was attributed to the doping of CQDs during the formation of PVDF films, which is a kind of particle with electronegativity on the surface. There exists a strong interaction between the hydroxyl on the CQDs and the F atoms in the PVDF at the PVDF/CQDs interface, and such interfacial interaction can promote local polarization of the PVDF molecular chains and induce the generation of the polar β-phase [55]. In addition, the doped CQDs can promote electron transfer and improve piezoelectric performance.

The PFM morphology, piezoelectric response amplitude, and phase diagrams of the hybrid films presented in Fig. 10 (a–c), which further confirms the piezoelectric properties of the prepared films. The piezoelectric hysteresis loop (Fig. 10(d)) showed that a 180° phase change was generated in the film under the effect of a 10 V reverse electric field. This indicated the existence of 180° domains in the hybrid film with a unique polarization transition process. The generation of a built-in piezoelectric field within the hybrid film was confirmed by the butterfly loop (Fig. 10(e)).

The reusability and stability of the film is crucial to its application in practical water treatment. Multi-cycle degradation experiments were carried out to explore the reusability of the hybrid films. The η of the hybrid film remained as high as 88.27 % after 10 times of dynamic degradation experiments (Fig. 11(a) ). Furthermore, the XPS (Fig. 11(b)) and SEM images (Fig. 11(c)) of the hybrid film before and after the cycling experiments showed almost no obvious changes, which indicated that the catalysts were still firmly immobilized on the film surface. The above results indicate the excellent reusability and stability of hybrid film, which exhibits great potential for practical applications.

Active radical trapping test and electron spin resonance (EPR) were employed to understand the catalytic active radicals. In Fig. 12 (a) and (b), no signal was detected in darkness, while the obvious DMPO/·O2 − and DMPO/·OH under visible light confirmed the existence of photoinduced active radicals of ·O2 − and ·OH. The free radical quenching experiments were carried out using potassium iodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA) and benzoquinone (BQ) as scavengers for h +, ·OH and ·O2 −, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 12(c), the η value was 95.26 % in the absence of the radical trapping agent, whereas η decreased to 73.38 %, 61.52 %, and 56.74 % with added BQ, KI, and IPA, respectively. The above results indicated that the effect of free radicals during RhB degradation follows ·OH > h +>·O2 −.

Total organic carbon (TOC) can facilitate the understanding of pollutant degradation mechanisms while characterizing the degree of mineralization. The results of the TOC test on RhB solution after catalytic degradation (Fig. 12(d)) showed that the hybrid film exhibited good mineralization ability for RhB. Furthermore, it can be found that TOC removal efficiencies were lower than catalytic degradation efficiency (η). In other words, the fully mineralization was behind the decolorized degradation of RhB. This suggested that some organic intermediates (e.g., aldehydes, carboxylic acids, etc.) may remain undegraded when the chromophore (aromatic ring) was completely destructed [56], which can be explained by the subsequent analysis of the degradation products and pathways of RhB.

To explore the pollutant degradation process during piezo-photocatalytic more clearly, the RhB structure was optimized and characterized by first-principles density functional theory (DFT). It can be seen that the RhB molecule consists of 64 atoms, including 28 carbon (C) atoms, 3 oxygen (O) atoms, 2 nitrogen (N) atoms, and 31 hydrogen (H) atoms (Fig. 13 (a)). The distribution of the electron cloud of RhB was reflected by the electrostatic potential (ESP) (Fig. 13(b)), in which the electron-rich regions were colored red and the electron-loss regions blue. The attack-vulnerable sites of RhB were located in the electron-rich regions of ESP. The HOMO and LUMO of the RhB molecule showed that the electron-rich regions were mainly located on the 8C, 12C, 21 N and 22 N atoms, while the LUMO concentrated on the 7C, 15C, 18C and 10O regions (Fig. 13(c) and (d)). In general, the HOMO orbitals reflect the regions where electrons can easily escape and are most vulnerable to the attack of the active radical. The HOMO orbitals were located mainly around the chromophore of the RhB molecule, suggesting that the chromophore was the main site of attack by active radicals. The reaction sites of the radicals with RhB molecules were analyzed in further detail by the Hirshfeld charge and the Fukui index (f +, f −, f 0) (Table S5). The sites with higher f +, f − and f 0 values (marked yellow in Table S5) were more favored to be attacked by active radicals during degradation. More specifically, ·O2 − as nucleophilic radicals tend to attack the sites with high f + values (15C, 30C, 27C, 31C, 11C, 20C), whereas the electrophilic h + would attack the sites with high f − (12C, 22 N, 2C, 21 N, 16C, 14C). The sites with high f 0 (26C, 27C, 30C, 31C, 8C, 12C) tend to be attacked by ·OH.

The intermediates of RhB catalytic degradation were probed by LC-MS (Fig. S6 and Table S6). The m/z were mainly concentrated around 387.17, 359.14, 361.14, 331.11, 241.06, 182.21, and 103.00, which indicated that the RhB molecule (m/z = 443.23) could be degraded and produced some intermediates in the piezo-photocatalytic process, and the large molecule intermediates could be eventually degraded into small molecules. Combined with TOC, DFT and LC-MS, the degradation products, degradation pathways and degradation mechanisms of RhB are shown in Fig. 14 (a). Firstly, the 21 N and 22 N positions of RhB were attacked by the active radical h +, leading to the delocalization of N-ethyl from the molecular structure and the generation of de-ethylated intermediates (P1, P2, P3, and P5). The P5 was allowed to be further oxidized to P4 by ·OH. Then, the chromophore structure of RhB was further attacked by reactive radical groups (·OH, H +, ·O2 −), leading to the decolorization of RhB (P6-P8). Further degradation destroyed the high molecular weight heteroanthracene and phenyl groups, as well as generating lower molecular weight acids by ring opening (P9-P11). These intermediates will eventually be mineralized to CO2, H2O, NO3 −, and NH4 +.

The dynamic piezo-photocatalytic degradation mechanism is depicted in Fig. 14(b). In detail, under visible light, the photoinduced hole-electron separation of TiO2 of the hybrid film. The photogenerated e − reacted with O2 to form ·O2 − since the CD potential (ECD) of CQDs (−1.14 eV vs. NHE) is more negative compared to that of O2/·O2 − (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [57,58]. Whereas the h + left on VB will oxidize H2O or OH− to ·OH due to the higher potential of the EVB of TiO2 (+2.49 eV vs. NHE) compared to OH−/·OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) and H2O/·OH (+2.27 eV vs. NHE) [59,60]. Moreover, h + with a strong oxidative capacity can react with pollutants directly. The electron trapping effects of the CQDs embedded within the PVDF film and TiO2, as well as the Ti-F coordination bonds provided multiple channels for photogenerated electron transfer. During the dynamic piezo-photocatalytic process, the charge transfer was facilitated by the IEF (IEF Ⅰ) established at the interface of S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures immobilized on the surface of the film. The IEF (IEF Ⅱ) based on piezoelectric potential generated by the flexible hybrid films under the strain caused by the flowing solution also provided a strong driving force for the migration of e − and h + (e − would be attracted to the positive level of the piezoelectric field, whereas h + would be repulsed to the opposite direction.). In the presence of the dual IEF, the transport of charge was greatly accelerated and electron-hole recombination was significantly inhibited. Thus, more effective e − and h + were generated. Finally, the hybrid film realized efficient removal of RhB under light and solution flow.

This study provided a facile method for the preparation of PVDF/CQDs-TiO2@CQDs hybrid films with piezo-photocatalytic properties. S-scheme TiO2@CQDs heterostructures were immobilized on the film surface and the ridge-like morphology ensured the exposure of adsorption and catalytic active sites. The CQDs embedded in the TiO2 and film matrix, as well as the Ti-F coordination bonds with the electron trapping effect provided nucleation growth sites for the in situ growth of TiO2 nanoparticles on the film surface, while multiple channels were established for electron transfer to improve photoactivity. The degradation properties of RhB were significantly enhanced by the piezo-photocatalytic synergistic effect based on dual built-in electric fields under flowing solution and visible light conditions (the η reached 95.26 % within 100 min, with a ks value of about 2.3 times that of static photocatalytic). The hybrid film exhibited excellent reusability and long-term stability over 10 reuse cycles. TOC, LC-MS and DFT theoretical calculations indicated that during the piezo-photocatalytic process, RhB was mainly attacked by ·OH, h + and ·O2 − and gradually decolorized and mineralized. In this study, morphology control, heterogeneous interface engineering and photo-electric synergistic catalysis strategies were introduced into the design and preparation of catalytic materials and film reactors, which provided a promising pathway for the fields of energy conversion and environmental remediation.
